museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Mario Bros. begins appearing in release records, the US console market keeps sagging, and British home-computer culture grows around Spectrum, BBC Micro and printed listings.
museum.curatorNote: Mario Bros. is a good example of 1983 dating trouble: some references cite March in North America, while Nintendo-associated sources often point to July in Japan. The exhibit preserves that uncertainty.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: March shows the difference between arcade invention and home-market doubt.

items.title: Mario Bros. appears in North American release records
items.body: Some arcade release references place Mario Bros. in North America in March, months before the better-known July Japanese date. The exact rollout is treated carefully here.
items.certainty: reported North American month; conflicting records
items.sourceNote: Mario Bros. references cite March 1983 North America and July 1983 Japan.

items.title: The crash becomes harder to ignore
items.body: By spring, the US console downturn was no longer just a poor Christmas story. Retailers, publishers and investors were all watching demand weaken.
items.sourceNote: Video game crash summaries describe severe market shrinkage through 1983.

items.title: UK magazines keep the arcade and micro worlds together
items.body: C&VG and the broader computing press let readers move from cabinet reviews to BASIC listings in one sitting.
items.sourceNote: World of Spectrum lists C&VG issue 17 for March 1983.

items.title: Home computers keep gaining credibility
items.body: The Spectrum, BBC Micro, VIC-20 and C64 were no longer peripheral to games. They were where new British work could appear quickly.

items.title: Arcade design keeps changing shape
items.body: Mario Bros. pointed toward enclosed-screen platform play: single-screen arenas, enemies with patterns, and a plumber becoming more than a cameo.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight titles and release-window objects around March's archive drawer.

items.title: Mario Bros.
items.body: Nintendo's pipe-and-platform game gave Mario and Luigi a shared stage and a new grammar of enemy flipping.
items.certainty: reported NA month; Japan date differs
items.sourceNote: Release records conflict between March North America and July Japan.

items.title: Popeye
items.body: Still a visible Nintendo cabinet with a cartoon-licence shine.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: A racing cabinet that still looked like premium technology.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: A reminder that arcade games could be odd, funny and visually distinct.

items.title: Spectrum cassette games
items.body: The commercial cassette shelf kept growing ahead of the summer's landmark UK releases.
items.sourceNote: Specific March title date not asserted.

items.title: The Hobbit
items.body: An adventure still acting as proof that home games could be literary, awkward and memorable.

items.title: Atari 2600 discount titles
items.body: The shelf increasingly mixed classics, rush jobs and heavily reduced stock.

items.title: Computer magazine type-ins
items.body: A playable game could still arrive as printed code rather than shrink-wrap.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The machines tell two different stories: arcade freshness and home uncertainty.

items.title: Nintendo arcade hardware
items.body: Mario Bros. showed Nintendo refining character action before the Famicom brought that work home in Japan.

items.title: Atari 2600 retail shelf
items.body: The installed base was enormous, but retail confidence was now visibly strained.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 48K
items.body: The version many UK players wanted for richer games, better screens and a growing cassette library.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: A games-capable computer gaining attention as the home-computer price fight intensified.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: March's magazine case is where release uncertainty becomes visible.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Issue 17: UK arcade and micro coverage
items.curatorNote: A verified March issue anchors the UK perspective: arcade play and home computing in the same paper world.
items.body: A verified March issue anchors the UK perspective: arcade play and home computing in the same paper world.
items.sourceNote: World of Spectrum lists C&VG issue 17, March 1983.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Arcades while the market shakes
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for March 1983.
items.curatorNote: US videogame magazines still had strong games to cover even as the business mood changed.
items.body: US videogame magazines still had strong games to cover even as the business mood changed.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The serious home machine as games machine
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for March 1983.
items.curatorNote: UK home-computer magazines made games feel part of learning, tinkering and ownership.
items.body: UK home-computer magazines made games feel part of learning, tinkering and ownership.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum listings and early software culture
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for spring 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum was becoming a platform with its own press identity.
items.body: The Spectrum was becoming a platform with its own press identity.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: March discovery was analogue and uneven.

items.title: Release dates were rumours
items.body: A game could be out in one region, reported in another, and unseen locally.

items.title: Print joined scenes together
items.body: A UK reader could learn about American arcade releases beside Spectrum code.

items.title: Tapes moved hand to hand
items.body: Commercial software, demos and typed-in experiments travelled socially.

items.title: Arcade knowledge was observational
items.body: You learned by watching someone survive one screen longer.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: March felt like the calendar could not keep up with the machines.

items.title: Mario was becoming a fixture
items.body: Not yet the global console mascot, but already recognisable beyond Donkey Kong.

items.title: The market felt noisy
items.body: Too many products made choosing harder, not easier.

items.title: UK micros felt personal
items.body: The bedroom machine belonged to you in a way a cabinet never could.

items.title: The magazine was the timeline
items.body: For many players, history arrived monthly, not instantly.

sources.note: Used for conflicting March North America and July Japan release references.

sources.note: Used for verified UK magazine issue context.
